pattern: Making every row palindromic and making every column palindromic are two completely independent goals, each costing one flip per mismatched mirrored pair, so the answer is simply the cheaper of the two totals.
hints: Making all rows palindromic doesn't require touching columns at all, and vice versa -- so compute the cost of each goal completely separately and take the smaller one.
For a single row (or column) to read the same forwards and backwards, every pair of cells equidistant from the two ends must match; whenever they don't, exactly one flip fixes that pair.
Sum the mismatched-pair count across every row for the 'rows' total, and across every column for the 'columns' total (a transposed pass works well here), then return the minimum of the two sums.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two flips make rows palindromic
statement example, one flip makes columns palindromic
statement example, already palindromic both ways
single row, only column direction can ever need work
single column, only row direction can ever need work
uniform grid, both directions already free
checkerboard where rows and columns need different amounts of work
odd width row so the middle column never needs a partner

stress.description: large random grids near the m*n budget with varied aspect ratios
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 400))
    max_n = max(1, 200000 // m)
    n = rng.randint(1, min(max_n, 400))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]
